script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Monday — here is your QQQ recap. The portfolio closed up zero point five six percent on the session. A mixed day across primary holdings, with several names moving sharply in opposite directions ahead of a critical earnings week.

Apple's federal lawsuit against OpenAI may be the most consequential legal filing in tech this year. On the surface, it reads as a trade secret dispute. One analyst argues it signals something far more alarming — that Apple spotted a direct threat inside OpenAI's hardware roadmap and moved to neutralize it before the broader market understood what was at stake.

United States equities closed slightly lower on the session as investors weighed Middle East tensions against optimism surrounding upcoming tech earnings. Trump's warning of additional strikes on Iran pressured risk sentiment and pushed crude oil higher. The S&P five hundred edged down on the day, while markets remained in a holding pattern ahead of major results due later this week.

Alphabet heads into Wednesday's second quarter earnings report carrying the weight of the entire tech rally on its shoulders. Analysts at Seeking Alpha flagged three critical questions heading into the print — artificial intelligence return on investment, capital expenditure trajectory, and whether cloud growth at Google can sustain the pace set in the first quarter. One market strategist was direct: to justify current valuations in names like Alphabet, earnings growth needs to clear a high bar — not a standard ten percent — but meaningfully above that figure with strong margin support alongside it. Expectations are running high. Alphabet gained one point five one percent on Monday's session, suggesting the market is positioning ahead of what could be a defining print for the entire sector.

Nvidia was up zero point two three percent on Monday's session, a quiet move for the portfolio's largest holding. The name continues to benefit from the broader artificial intelligence memory boom, though one analyst note cautioned investors not to mistake the current cycle for a permanent supercycle — history suggests these expansions have limits. Worth watching closely as memory sector commentary builds into the back half of the week.

Apple was down two point one four percent on Monday's session, one of the session's sharper declines among primary holdings. Two distinct narratives are running simultaneously. The lawsuit against OpenAI drew significant analyst attention, with one read suggesting it signals a forthcoming hardware war rather than a straightforward legal dispute. Separately, Apple faces a margin test into its own earnings, with strong services growth viewed as the likely offset to weaker product margins. Both threads will define how the street receives the next print.

Alphabet, as covered in the top story, gained one point five one percent. Turning to Micron Technology — up one point nine four percent on Monday's session. The memory sector commentary tied to the artificial intelligence build-out remains constructive, though the same historical cycle warning that applies to Nvidia applies here. Microsoft gained two point one five percent, the strongest performer among primary holdings in Monday's session. Bank of America reiterated confidence in the name ahead of earnings despite the stock being one of the worst performers among large-cap technology names in twenty twenty-six, having shed roughly one-fifth of its value this year even as Azure growth continued.

Over at Amazon, up one point one two percent on Monday's session. One analysis reframed the valuation entirely around silicon — arguing that Wall Street is still pricing Amazon like a retailer when the more relevant driver is its semiconductor and infrastructure positioning. That framing, if it gains traction, changes the earnings conversation meaningfully.

Advanced Micro Devices gained one point five eight percent on Monday's session. No single-name catalyst drove the move — the name tracked broader semiconductor strength. Tesla was down two point nine six percent, the weakest performer among primary holdings in Monday's session. Analysts flagged the stock specifically when discussing valuations that require above-average earnings growth to justify current multiples. Results are due later this week and will be closely watched. Intel gained two point one three percent on Monday's session. Like Alphabet, Intel is in the earnings queue — results are expected to provide a read on corporate health across the semiconductor supply chain.

Looking at secondary holdings, Sandisk gained two point six seven percent in Monday's session and Broadcom added one point nine eight percent — both outperforming the primary field. SpaceX fell three point three four percent, the steepest decline across all holdings. The Wall Street Journal reported the company is in talks to sell artificial intelligence computing capacity to the Defense Department, which could matter for the longer-term thesis even as the stock pulled back sharply.

Three items to track in the next forty-eight hours. First, Alphabet reports second quarter earnings on Wednesday, July twenty-second — the result will either validate or pressure the entire artificial intelligence investment thesis across this portfolio. Second, Tesla reports this week as well — margins and delivery guidance will be the critical data points for holders. Third, Intel's earnings are in the queue and will offer a read on semiconductor demand trends that touches multiple names in this portfolio directly.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Watch Alphabet's Wednesday print closely — it sets the tone for everything that follows this earnings season.
